claims.flip_evidence_text: provided, further, however, that any effect, change, event or occurrence referred to in clause (a), (b), (c) or (d) may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect to the extent such effect, change, event or occurrence has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate
claims.notes: The claim accurately reflects the carve-out in clause (b) and the disproportionate-impact exception in the final proviso.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any fact, effect, change, event, circumstance, occurrence or development that, individually or in the aggregate, has or is reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on the business, assets, liabilities, condition (financial or otherwise) or results of operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole; provided, however, that in no event shall any of the following, alone or in combination, be deemed to constitute, nor be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a Company Material Adverse Effect: (a) changes within or affecting the general property and casualty insurance industry, the United States or global economy, or changes within or affecting global or United States economic or capital market conditions (including changes in the credit, debt, financial, or currency markets), (b) changes in or adoption of any applicable Laws or applicable accounting regulations or principles or interpretations thereof (including changes in GAAP or in SAP prescribed or permitted by the applicable Insurance Regulators and accounting pronouncements by the SEC, the National Association of Insurance Commissioners and the Financial Accounting Standards Board), (c) changes in global or national political conditions (including political action or inaction, the outbreak or escalation of war, military action, insurrection, sabotage or acts of terrorism), (d) changes due to any acts of God, natural disasters, man-made disasters, disease outbreak, global public health emergency (as declared by the World Health Organization), epidemics or pandemics (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof or any COVID-19 Measures), (e) change, event, effect, development or circumstance arising out of the announcement of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby or the pendency of the Merger or the identity of the parties to this Agreement, including any termination of, reduction in or similar negative impact on relationships, contractual or otherwise, with any Governmental Authorities or any customers, suppliers, reinsurers, agents, policyholders, partners, officers or employees of the Company and its Subsidiaries; provided, however, that this clause (e) shall not apply with respect to the representations and warranties made by the Company in Section 4.05 of this Agreement, (f) actions taken or omitted to be taken pursuant to the express provisions of this Agreement to obtain any consent, approval, authorization or waiver under applicable Law in connection with the Merger and the other transactions contemplated hereby, (g) the entering into and performance of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, including compliance with the covenants set forth herein, or any action taken or omitted to be taken by the Company at the express written request or with the prior written consent of Parent or Merger Sub, (h) the effects of any breach, violation or non- performance of any provision of this Agreement by Parent or any of its Affiliates, (i) any initiated or threatened Action against the Company, any of its Affiliates or any of their respective directors or officers arising out of this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby, (j) any failure in and of itself (but not the underlying cause thereof) by the Company to meet any published analyst estimates or expectations of the Company’s revenues, premiums written, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations for any period or any failure in and of itself (but not the underlying cause thereof) by the Company to meet its internal or published projections, budgets, plans or forecasts of its revenues, premiums written, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations or any change in the price or trading volume of the Company Common Shares, (k) changes in the value of the investment assets owned by a Company Insurance Subsidiary (but not the underlying cause thereof) or (l) changes or developments in and of itself (but not the underlying cause thereof) in the credit, financial strength or other rating of the Company, any of its Subsidiaries or its outstanding debt, (m) any effect, change, event, occurrence or circumstance arising out of, resulting from or related to the matters described in Section 1.01A of the Company Disclosure Letter; provided, further, however, that any effect, change, event or occurrence referred to in clause (a), (b), (c) or (d) may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect to the extent such effect, change, event or occurrence has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate (in which case the incremental disproportionate effect or effects may be taken into account in determining whether or not a Company Material Adverse Effect has occurred). (Page 10)

Question:
Change in law: subject to "disproportionate impact" modifier

Llama answer:
A change in law, as described in clause (b) of the definition of "Company Material Adverse Effect", will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless it has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate.

Canonical claims:
- claim-70d16ce43960: A change in law, as described in clause (b) of the definition of "Company Material Adverse Effect", will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless it has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt, a question, and a model answer (Llama answer). I need to return for each claim:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Claim: claim-70d16ce43960: "A change in law, as described in clause (b) of the definition of "Company Material Adverse Effect", will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless it has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate."

Contract excerpt analysis:
- Clause (b) says changes in/adoption of applicable Laws/accounting regulations are excluded from being deemed a CMAE or taken into account in determining whether there has been/will be a CMAE.
- However, the proviso at the end says: "provided, further, however, that any effect, change, event or occurrence referred to in clause (a), (b), (c) or (d) may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect to the extent such effect, change, event or occurrence has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate (in which case the incremental disproportionate effect or effects may be taken into account in determining whether or not a Company Material Adverse Effect has occurred)."

So the claim says: A change in law... will not be considered a CMAE unless it has a disproportionate adverse effect...
This is essentially correct. The carve-out in (b) applies, but the final proviso allows it to be taken into account if disproportionate.

Wait, is it fully true? Let's parse carefully.
The claim says: "will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless it has a disproportionate adverse effect..."
The text says clause (b) items shall not be deemed to constitute, nor be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a CMAE... BUT the final proviso says they MAY be taken into account to the extent of disproportionate adverse effect.

So yes, the default is that it's excluded, unless the disproportionate impact exception applies. The claim captures this correctly.

Is it load-bearing? The claim is essentially identical to the Llama answer. The question is "Change in law: subject to 'disproportionate impact' modifier". The claim restates the Llama answer. Since it's the core assertion being evaluated, and if we flip it, it would change the meaning completely. But wait, what is the "load_bearing_label" criteria? Typically, a claim is load-bearing if it is a central factual or legal assertion that, if false, would undermine the answer. This claim is essentially the entire answer. So yes, it's load-bearing.

Flip evidence text: If the claim were false, what would the evidence be? The contract text showing that changes in law are excluded PERIOD, without disproportionate impact exception; or that they are always included. The actual text shows the disproportionate impact exception exists for clauses (a)-(d). So the flip evidence would be the parts of the text showing either that (b) is excluded without any disproportionate exception, or that (b) is never excluded. But we need to provide text from the contract that would support the opposite. Actually, the flip evidence should be the text that contradicts the claim. Since the claim says "unless disproportionate", the flip would be "even if disproportionate" or "always excluded". The contract says: "provided, however, that in no event shall any of the following... (b) ... be deemed to constitute, nor be taken into account..." and then the final proviso allows it for disproportionate. So the flip evidence would be the final proviso? No, that supports the claim.

Wait, I need to think about what "flip_evidence_text" means. It usually means the evidence text that would make the claim false if it were the case, i.e., the contrasting evidence in the document. But since the claim is true, the flip evidence would be the text that, if it were the only text, would make it false? Actually, in these tasks, if load_bearing_label=yes, we need to provide the text from the contract that, if the claim were false, would be the basis for that determination, or perhaps the text that most directly supports the opposite. More commonly, it's the specific evidence text that the claim relies on, but phrased as the flip? No, I think "flip_evidence_text" is the text from the source that would contradict the claim if the claim were false. Let me reconsider.

Actually, looking at similar annotation tasks: "flip_evidence_text" is often the specific sentence or phrase in the source text that, if interpreted differently or if it were different, would flip the label. But since the label is true, the flip evidence is the text that contains the condition that makes the claim true? No, that doesn't make sense.

Let me re-read instructions: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". Usually in these tasks, if a claim is load-bearing, you need to provide the specific evidence text from the contract that supports the claim (or that would be used to verify it). But the name "flip" suggests it's the evidence that, if the claim were false, would be the counter-evidence. However, often in these schemas, "flip_evidence_text" is actually the evidence text that the claim is based on (the "load bearing" evidence). Wait, no.

Let me think. In some legal annotation frameworks:
- correctness_label: whether the claim is supported by the source.
- load_bearing_label: whether the claim is central to the answer.
- flip_evidence_text: the specific text from the source that the claim is based on (the evidence). The term "flip" might mean that if this evidence were absent or opposite, the claim would flip.

Actually, I recall a specific task format from a paper or product where "flip_evidence_text" is the minimal span that, if changed, would flip the truth value of the claim. Or it's the span that most directly supports the claim.

Let me look at the claim and contract. The claim is about clause (b) and the disproportionate impact modifier. The relevant contract text is:
"provided, however, that in no event shall any of the following, alone or in combination, be deemed to constitute, nor be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a Company Material Adverse Effect: ... (b) changes in or adoption of any applicable Laws ... ; provided, further, however, that any effect, change, event or occurrence referred to in clause (a), (b), (c) or (d) may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect to the extent such effect, change, event or occurrence has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate..."

So the flip evidence text should be the final proviso about disproportionate effect applying to (a)-(d). Because that's the key text that makes the claim true (or if absent, the claim would be false). So I'll provide that text.

Wait, is the claim "partially_true"? Let's double-check.
Claim: "A change in law, as described in clause (b) ... will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless it has a disproportionate adverse effect..."
The text says changes in law are excluded from being deemed a CMAE AND from being taken into account, EXCEPT that they may be taken into account if disproportionate.
Does "will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless..." accurately capture this?
If a change in law has a disproportionate effect, it MAY be taken into account. But does that mean it WILL be considered a CMAE? No, it just means the incremental disproportionate effect may be taken into account. It doesn't automatically make it a CMAE; it's just not excluded from consideration. The claim says "will not be considered ... unless". This is slightly imprecise because even with disproportionate effect, it's not automatically a CMAE; it's just that the exclusion doesn't apply to the disproportionate part. However, in common parlance for these annotations, this is usually considered true.

Let's look at the exact wording of the Llama answer, which is identical to the claim. The question asks about the "disproportionate impact" modifier. The answer/claim captures the gist: change in law is excluded, unless disproportionate. This is true.

Is there any nuance? The claim says "will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless..." The contract says "in no event shall any of the following ... be deemed to constitute ... a Company Material Adverse Effect". So they are excluded from being deemed a CMAE. The final proviso says they "may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect". So they can be considered in the determination, but only the incremental disproportionate effect. The claim says "unless it has a disproportionate adverse effect". This is a reasonable summary. I think "true" is appropriate.

Load-bearing: Yes, because it's the main point of the answer.

Flip evidence text: I'll quote the final proviso that applies to (a)-(d), specifically mentioning (b).

Let's craft the exact text:
"provided, further, however, that any effect, change, event or occurrence referred to in clause (a), (b), (c) or (d) may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect to the extent such effect, change, event or occurrence has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Company and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other participants operating in the industries and geographies in which the Company and its Subsidiaries operate"

I should make sure to include the relevant part that mentions (b). That's sufficient.

Notes: optional. I can leave empty or add a brief note about the incremental disproportionate effect nuance if I thought it was partially_true, but I think true is fine. I'll leave a short note noting that the claim captures the carve-out and exception accurately.

Wait, let me reconsider if it should be partially_true. The claim says "will not be considered ... unless". The contract says "in no event shall ... be deemed to constitute ... provided further that ... may be taken into account ...". The language "deemed to constitute" vs "taken into account" is slightly different. The exclusion says they shall not be deemed to constitute a CMAE. The proviso says they may be taken into account to the extent of disproportionate effect. Does "taken into account" mean "considered a CMAE"? Not exactly; it means it can be part of the analysis. However, for the purpose of determining whether there is a CMAE, if the disproportionate effect is taken into account and tips the balance, then it leads to a CMAE. So "unless" is a fair shorthand.

Given that these annotations usually accept reasonable paraphrases, "true" is correct.

One more check: The claim says "A change in law, as described in clause (b) ... will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless..." This is exactly what the Llama answer says. The question asks "Change in law: subject to 'disproportionate impact' modifier". The answer is directly responsive. Yes.

Final check on flip_evidence_text: Should I include the exclusion text too? The flip evidence is the text that, if it were different, would flip the claim. The claim is true because of the final proviso. If the final proviso didn't exist, the claim would be false. So the final proviso is the load-bearing evidence. I'll provide that.

Actually, some schemas want the text that directly supports the claim (i.e., the evidence). The term "flip" is confusing. But I'll provide the proviso text.

Let's format the output.

The proviso says they may be taken into account to the extent of disproportionate effect. Does "taken into account" mean "considered a CMAE"? Not exactly; it means it can be part of the analysis. However, for the purpose of determining whether there is a CMAE, if the disproportionate effect is taken into account and tips the balance, then it leads to a CMAE. So "unless" is a fair shorthand.

Given that these annotations usually accept reasonable paraphrases, "true" is correct.

One more check: The claim says "A change in law, as described in clause (b) ... will not be considered a Company Material Adverse Effect unless..." This is exactly what the Llama answer says. The question asks "Change in law: subject to 'disproportionate impact' modifier". The answer is directly responsive. Yes.

Final check on flip_evidence_text: Should I include the exclusion text too? The flip evidence is the text that, if it were different, would flip the claim.
